ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a dedicated wholemeal wrap entry; closest defensible match is whole wheat flatbread (FOODON_03315830). Code used as approximate anchor only.
ontology.foodex2_description: Bread and similar products — whole grain / wholemeal bread
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific wholemeal wrap subcategory; A000E (whole grain bread) is the closest defensible parent node.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No sufficiently specific SNOMED CT code identified for wholemeal wrap as a distinct food concept; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium wholemeal wrap (approximately 60 g) is a typical single serving used as a wrap or flatbread base for a meal.

nutrient_source.title: Wholemeal Wrap — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per_100g fields. Values preserved as reported. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for whole wheat tortilla (FDC ID 168012) as the closest approved secondary source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Wholemeal / Whole Wheat Flatbread — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 69 (medium) is consistent with published University of Sydney GI Database values for whole wheat / wholemeal flatbreads and wraps. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (69 × 50.63 × 0.60) / 100 = (69 × 30.38) / 100 ≈ 20.96, rounded to 20.9. Input record cited 'Embedded GI Database' at confidence 0.703; value cross-referenced against University of Sydney GI Database analogous whole wheat flatbread entries and accepted as defensible.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 845 mg per 100 g — substantially exceeds the 600 mg/100 g threshold for hypertension concern
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should avoid or strictly limit wholemeal wraps due to the very high sodium content of 845 mg/100 g; if consumed, restrict to half a wrap (≈30 g, contributing ~254 mg sodium) and pair with potassium-rich fillings such as spinach, tomato, or avocado to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (69) with 50.6 g carbohydrate per 100 g, partially moderated by 3.1 g dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may include one wholemeal wrap (60 g, ~30 g carbohydrate) per meal with portion control, pairing it with low-GI vegetables and lean protein to blunt the postprandial glycaemic response; high-sugar fillings and sauces should be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (845 mg/100 g) is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is low (~1.5 g/100 g) and wholegrain fibre (3.1 g/100 g) is beneficial
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The wholegrain fibre content of wholemeal wraps supports LDL cholesterol reduction, but the high sodium content (845 mg/100 g) poses a meaningful cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to half a wrap and seek low-sodium alternatives where possible.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Dietary fibre (3.1 g/100 g) supports gut microbiome and anti-inflammatory pathways, but high sodium (845 mg/100 g) may exacerbate tissue oedema
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Wholemeal wraps may be consumed in moderation by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis given the wholegrain fibre benefit, but portion size should be limited to reduce sodium-driven fluid retention; check ingredient labels for partially hydrogenated oils which would introduce pro-inflammatory trans fats.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A wholemeal wrap is a soft, pliable flatbread made primarily from whole wheat (wholemeal) flour, water, vegetable oil, salt, and leavening agents. It is commercially produced through a baking or griddle process and is widely available in Singapore supermarkets and food service outlets. The texture is soft and slightly chewy, with a mild nutty flavour from the whole wheat flour. Wholemeal wraps are used as a versatile base for sandwiches, burritos, and meal wraps, filled with vegetables, proteins, and sauces. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides 311 kcal, 8.2 g protein, 7.6 g fat, and 50.6 g carbohydrate with 3.1 g dietary fibre. The wholegrain base contributes beneficial fibre that supports digestive health and moderates postprandial blood glucose. However, the sodium content of 845 mg/100 g is notably high due to salt used in commercial production, making this food unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease unless consumed in very small portions. The glycemic index is approximately 69 (medium), and a standard 60 g serving delivers approximately 30 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of around 20.9. Wholemeal wraps are suitable for vegetarians and vegans and are generally halal-certified in Singapore. They are commonly consumed at breakfast, lunch, or as a light dinner base.

Is wholemeal wrap good for diabetes?
Can I eat wholemeal wrap if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of a wholemeal wrap?

Wholemeal wrap vs white wrap nutrition
How much fibre in a wholemeal wrap?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy whole wheat tortilla as the closest approved secondary source. GI value of 69 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database analogous whole wheat flatbread entries. All values are internally consistent and nutritionally plausible.
notes: Saturated fat (1.5 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for whole wheat tortilla (FDC ID 168012) as HPB did not report this field for this entry. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these fields are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for wholemeal wrap and detailed micronutrient data for this specific commercial product is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026. GI source cited as 'Embedded GI Database' in input at confidence 0.703; cross-referenced against University of Sydney GI Database analogous whole wheat flatbread entries and accepted as defensible at GI 69 (medium). This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; it is an internationally produced staple, so no mandatory local-language names apply under G6 multilingual rules. Ontology codes are approximate anchors only — no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for wholemeal wrap as a distinct subcategory.
